1: David assembles thirty thousand chosen men (kol-bĕḥûr bĕyiśrāʾēl šĕlōšîm ʾālep̄) for the ark transport. The number thirty thousand — the same as the Israelite levy that died with the ark in 1 Samuel 4:10 — is not coincidental: this is the full covenant assembly, the restoration of what was lost when the ark was captured. The scale signals that this is not a military expedition but a covenant ceremony of national importance.
2: David sets out ‘from Baale-judah’ (= Kiriath-jearim, 1 Chronicles 13:6) to bring up the ark of God, ‘which is called by the name, the name of YHWH of hosts who sits enthroned on the cherubim.’ The full divine name formula attached to the ark — YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôṯ yōšēḇ hakkĕrūḇîm — identifies the ark as YHWH’s throne-presence. The cherubim-enthroned YHWH is the divine warrior king seated between the golden cherubim of the mercy seat; bringing the ark to Jerusalem is bringing the divine king to his royal city.
3: They place the ark on a new cart (ʿăgālāh ḥăḏāšāh) — precisely the mistake that the previous ark transport made (1 Samuel 6:7: the Philistines used a new cart). The Mosaic law specified that the Kohathites were to carry the ark on their shoulders with poles (Numbers 4:15; 2 Samuel 7:9); using a cart was the Philistine method, adapted from the Philistine return. The use of the ‘new cart’ signals that David’s first attempt is following Philistine protocol rather than Mosaic prescription — a syncretism that will prove deadly.
4: Uzzah and Ahio drive the cart from Abinadab’s house. Ahio walks in front (lĕp̄ānāyw) of the ark — the proper ceremonial position for the procession leader. The narrative’s double-mention of the cart and the personnel (vv3-4) sets up the tension: everything looks correct — new cart, authorized handlers, celebratory procession — but the method is wrong, and the theological crisis will reveal the error.
5: David and all Israel celebrate before YHWH with all kinds of instruments — bĕkol-ʿăṣê bĕrôšîm wĕḇĕkinnōrôṯ ûḇĕnĕḇālîm ûḇĕṯuppîm ûḇĕmĕnāʿanĕʿîm ûḇĕṣeliṣalîm (fir/cypress wood instruments, lyres, harps, tambourines, rattles, cymbals). The comprehensive musical listing parallels the Psalter’s instrument inventories (Ps 150). The contrast between the celebratory atmosphere and the imminent crisis underscores the narrative’s theological point: sincere enthusiasm is not the same as covenant obedience.
6: When they reach the threshing floor of Nacon (gōren nāḵôn), the oxen stumble (šāmĕṭû, H8058 — slipped/stumbled) and Uzzah reaches out and grabs the ark of God. The verb ʾāḥaz (H270, to seize/grab) describes an act of apparent service — preventing the ark from falling. The problem is that the touching itself is the violation: Numbers 4:15 explicitly prohibits the Kohathites from touching the holy things on pain of death (lōʾ-yiggĕʿû ʾel-haqqōḏeš wāmēṯû). The ark’s holiness is not conditional on whether it is in danger of falling.
7: wayyiḥar-ʾap̄ YHWH bĕʿuzzāh wayyakkēhû šām hāʾĕlōhîm ʿal-haššal — ‘The anger of YHWH flared against Uzzah, and God struck him down there for his irreverence (ʾal-haššal).’ The term šal (H7944, a hapax legomenon — irreverence, audacity, mishandling) is unique; interpretations range from ‘rashness’ to ‘the mistake.’ Whatever the precise nuance, the death is covenantal: YHWH’s holiness is not suspended by good intentions. The ark is YHWH’s holy throne; no human hand may touch it. The severity of the response establishes that covenant protocol cannot be improvised.
8: David is angry (wayyiḥar lĕdāwid) at YHWH’s striking of Uzzah. The same verb (ḥārah, H2734, to burn/flare with anger) used for YHWH’s anger in v7 is now used for David’s anger — a mirror structure. The name given: Perez-Uzzah (‘the breaking-out against Uzzah’, from pāraṣ, to break through) — the same pāraṣ David had just used in praise (v20: ‘YHWH has burst through my enemies like a bursting of waters’). YHWH’s pāraṣ in battle is victory; YHWH’s pāraṣ against Uzzah is judgment. The same divine attribute operates in both directions.
9: David is afraid of YHWH that day (wayyîrāʾ dāwid ʾet-YHWH) and asks: ‘How can the ark of YHWH come to me?’ The yārēʾ (fear) here is the appropriate covenant response to YHWH’s holiness in action — not terror that paralyzes but the reverential recognition that YHWH is holy and the approach to him must be on his terms. Qohelet’s summary: ‘Fear God and keep his commandments’ (Ecclesiastes 12:13). David’s question is the right question: on what terms can the holy God come to me? The answer will require learning the proper transport method.
10: David diverts the ark to the house of Obed-edom the Gittite (ʾeḏôm haggittî). The Gittite identification (from Gath) — a foreigner from Philistine territory — is notable: the ark that drove the Philistines to disaster (1 Sam 5-6) now blesses a Gittite’s household. The same divine presence that is dangerous when approached incorrectly is a source of extraordinary blessing when hosted properly. The covenant pattern: YHWH’s holiness is not domesticated by association with the foreigner who receives it rightly.
11: The ark stays at Obed-edom’s house for three months, and YHWH blesses his household and everything he has. The three-month blessing of Obed-edom while David has withdrawn in fear demonstrates what the ark’s presence was meant to produce: covenant blessing for the covenant community. The blessing that should have come to Jerusalem via David is instead coming to a Gittite’s house — a pointed reversal that motivates David’s second attempt with proper protocol.
12: wayyuggaḏ lammelek dāwid lēʾmōr bēraḵ YHWH ʾet-bêṯ ʾōḇēḏ-ʾeḏôm wĕʾet-kol-ʾăšer-lô baʿăḇûr ʾărôn hāʾĕlōhîm — ‘YHWH has blessed the household of Obed-edom and everything belonging to him, on account of the ark of God.’ The reported blessing motivates David’s return for the ark. His going to bring up the ark from Obed-edom’s house ‘with joy’ (bĕśimḥāh) — this time, 1 Chronicles 15:2 records, with Levites carrying it on their shoulders as Moses commanded. The text here does not specify the change of protocol; the Chronicles parallel provides the correction that explains the successful completion.
13: When those carrying the ark had gone six paces, David sacrificed an ox and a fattened calf. The sacrificial offering at the six-pace mark — a near-immediate offering after the procession begins successfully — marks the contrast with the previous failed attempt. The six-pace sacrifice is a thanksgiving that the ark is moving properly. The number six may be symbolic (creation week minus Sabbath rest) or simply the safe-distance that confirms YHWH’s acceptance of the revised protocol. David’s immediate offering connects the joyful procession to the sacrificial system.
14: wĕdāwid mĕkarker bĕkol-ʿōz lip̄nê YHWH wĕdāwid ḥāgûr ʾēp̄ôḏ bāḏ — ‘David danced before YHWH with all his strength, wearing a linen ephod.’ Kārar (H3769, to whirl/dance in a circle) is an intense, full-bodied dance expression; ‘with all his strength’ (bĕkol-ʿōz) emphasizes complete physical investment. The linen ephod (ʾēp̄ôḏ bāḏ) is the priestly garment — not the elaborate high-priestly ephod of Exod 28 but the simpler linen garment of priestly service (cf. Samuel in 1 Samuel 2:18). David dances in priestly garb, blurring the royal-priestly distinction in a way that anticipates the Psalm 110 melchizedekian priest-king.
15: All the house of Israel brings up the ark with shouting (bîrûʿāh, H8643 — the covenant war-shout that is simultaneously a liturgical acclaim) and the sound of the ram’s horn (šôp̄ār, H7782). The tĕrûʿāh (shout) is the sound of YHWH’s kingship — used at Sinai (Exodus 19:19: the sound of the trumpet), at Jericho (Joshua 6:5), and throughout the Psalms of Ascent. The ark’s entrance into Zion with this shout is the original of the Psalm 24 liturgy: ‘Lift up your heads, O gates... that the King of glory may come in. Who is this King of glory? YHWH of hosts — he is the King of glory!’
16: Michal daughter of Saul looks down through the window and sees David leaping and dancing before YHWH, and she despises him in her heart (wattiḇez lô bĕlibbāh). The window-gazing Michal seeing the king and despising him is a visual rhyme with 1 Samuel 18:28 where the verb of seeing and loving was used of her: the love has become contempt. Bāzāh (H959, to despise/treat with contempt) — the same verb used for Esau’s contempt of his birthright (Genesis 25:34), for despising YHWH’s offering (1 Samuel 2:17). The daughter of Saul applies Saul’s characteristic contempt for covenant worship to David’s priestly dance.
17: The ark is set in its place inside the tent David had pitched for it, and David offers burnt offerings and peace offerings before YHWH. The narrative sequence: ark installed → sacrifices offered → blessing of the people (v18) → distribution of bread, meat, and raisins (v19) → departure. This is the covenant feast structure: sanctuary installation, sacrifice, blessing, shared meal. It mirrors the covenant meal at Sinai (Exodus 24:9-11: the elders saw God and ate and drank) and anticipates the Psalm 22 promise: ‘Those who fear him will praise him... the afflicted shall eat and be satisfied’ (Psalms 22:24-26).
18: waykǎl dāwid mehāʿălôṯ hāʿōlāh wĕhašĕlāmîm wayyĕḇāreḵ ʾet-hāʿām bĕšēm YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôṯ — ‘When David had finished offering burnt offerings and peace offerings, he blessed the people in the name of YHWH of hosts.’ The priestly blessing of the people — ‘in the name of YHWH of hosts’ — is the kingly-priestly function David exercises: the king who blesses the covenant community through the divine name. Numbers 6:22-27: the Aaronic blessing ends with ‘they shall put my name on the Israelites, and I will bless them.’ David’s blessing mediates YHWH’s name to the assembled covenant community.
19: David distributes to every person in Israel — men and women alike — a round loaf of bread (ḥallat-leḥem), a portion of roasted meat (ʾešpār), and a raisin cake (ʾăšîšāh). The distribution to ‘men and women alike’ (lĕkol-hāʾām lĕkol-hāmôn yiśrāʾēl) — the entire covenant community without distinction — is the covenant meal as universal distribution. The three food items: bread (the staple of covenant life), meat (the sacrificial portion), and raisins (the fruit of the promised land). The king distributing the covenant feast to the entire people is the pattern that Jesus executes in the feeding of the five thousand (John 6:11) and formalizes in the Last Supper.
20: David returns to bless his household; Michal comes out to meet him and says: ‘How the king of Israel distinguished himself today — exposing himself to the eyes of slave girls as any shameless person might do!’ Qālal (H7043, to treat as light/contemptible/curse) — to make oneself light, low, despicable. Michal’s accusation is that David’s undignified dance qillēl (made contemptible) the royal office. She uses the language of ritual impurity and social shame. Her contempt is for the form of the worship rather than its substance.
21: David’s response: ‘It was before YHWH, who chose me (bāḥar bî) rather than your father and his whole house, to appoint me as ruler (nāgîḏ) over YHWH’s people Israel — I will celebrate before YHWH.’ The covenantal grounding of the dance: before YHWH who chose me. David’s worship is the response to divine election, not a performance for human approval. The contrast with Saul’s house is explicit: YHWH chose David rather than Saul. The nāgîḏ designation is deliberately used — YHWH’s designated-one, not the self-asserting king.
22: ‘I will make myself yet more contemptible (qālôṯî, same root as Michal’s accusation) than this, and I will be humble in my own sight.” David accepts Michal’s accusation and doubles it: yes, he will be even more self-lowering. The šāp̄āl (to be low/humble in one’s own eyes) is the posture of Deuteronomy 17:20 for the king — not to lift his heart above his brothers. David’s willingness to be seen as undignified before YHWH is the embodiment of the covenant king who serves YHWH rather than his own royal image. ‘But by the slave girls you spoke of — by them I will be honored.’ Those who witness the dance from below honor him precisely because of what Michal despises.
23: Michal daughter of Saul had no child to the day of her death (lōʾ-hāyāh lāh yeleḏ ʿaḏ yôm môṯāh). The barrenness as covenant judgment on her contempt for YHWH’s worship closes the Michal-Saul contrast: the daughter who despised the ark’s arrival with the royal contempt of her father’s house receives the covenant curse of childlessness that terminates Saul’s dynastic connection through David. The Saulide line through Michal ends; the Davidic line continues through others. The narrative of the barren woman has two sides: Hannah’s barrenness lifted when she consecrated her son; Michal’s barrenness is imposed because she despised covenant worship.
